lecture_context.lecture_title: Stanford CS224N: NLP with Deep Learning | Spring 2024 | Lecture 5 - Recurrent Neural Networks

unit.name: N-gram smoothing, backoff, and sampling
unit.description: Develops the main weaknesses of count-based language models and shows how they generate text by repeated sampling.
unit.summary: This segment focuses on the weaknesses of raw n-gram counts: unseen continuations get probability zero, unseen conditioning contexts make denominators undefined, and larger contexts worsen both sparsity and storage exponentially [ts=2345s] [ts=2433s] [ts=2520s]. The fixes are smoothing by adding a small delta to counts and backoff to shorter contexts when a longer one is missing [ts=2445s] [ts=2487s]. The instructor then shows how an n-gram model samples words one by one to generate text, producing output that is often grammatical but still globally incoherent, and argues that better models, not just more scale, are needed [ts=2752s] [ts=2838s] [ts=3017s].

key_points.text: Unseen n-grams create zero probabilities, which are especially damaging because downstream probability computations collapse to zero.
Add-delta smoothing assigns a small nonzero count to unseen events so every continuation remains possible.
Backoff replaces an unseen longer context with a shorter one until an estimate is available.
Using more conditioning words improves context but worsens sparsity and storage exponentially, so practice usually stopped around 5-grams.
N-gram text generation samples from the next-word distribution repeatedly to roll out a sequence.
Sampled n-gram text can look locally grammatical while remaining semantically incoherent.

kp_catalog.name: N-gram language models
kp_catalog.description: Count-based next-word models that use a short Markov context, smoothing, and backoff.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because n-gram language models clarifies count-based next-word models that use a short Markov context, smoothing, and backoff, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Autoregressive language modeling
kp_catalog.description: Training a model to predict the next token from its prefix, used directly for decoder pretraining and as a baseline pretraining objective more broadly. Autoregressive models make sequence modeling tractable by factoring p(x) into conditional next-part distributions over an ordered decomposition of x.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because autoregressive language modeling formalizes how training a model to predict the next token from its prefix, used directly for decoder pretraining and as a baseline pretraining objective more broadly, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.
